title: Papas Got A Brand New Excuse

jokes.text: Man, you ever feel like you was being watched? / All the time. When you look this good, you have to get used to it.
jokes.setup: Will's paranoid observation about being watched
jokes.punchline: Carlton's vain misinterpretation about his looks
jokes.explanation: Classic Carlton narcissism perfectly deployed. Great character integration - only Carlton would interpret surveillance as admiration for his looks.

jokes.text: When you talk that stupid, you have to get used to this, man: [Will presumably hits Carlton]
jokes.setup: Carlton's oblivious vanity
jokes.punchline: Will's physical retaliation
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick follow-up to Carlton's vanity. Functional but predictable physical comedy.

jokes.text: See that dude over there? He been scoping me since he got here. I think he's a cop. / Turn yourself in, Will. It's the only way.
jokes.setup: Will's concern about potential police surveillance
jokes.punchline: Carlton's immediate assumption of Will's guilt and dramatic advice
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Carlton's naive worldview leads to overdramatic 'turn yourself in' advice, showing his disconnect from Will's street perspective.

jokes.text: You're right. You're right. [pause] Wait a minute, man. I ain't do nothing.
jokes.setup: Will initially agrees with Carlton's advice
jokes.punchline: Delayed realization that he's innocent
jokes.explanation: Nice beat where Will gets caught up in Carlton's paranoia before remembering his actual innocence.

jokes.text: Uncle Lou, it's me, Carlton. Did you bring me anything?
jokes.setup: Carlton meeting his uncle for the first time in years
jokes.punchline: Immediately asking for presents
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton moment - his materialistic nature overrides social awareness in an emotionally charged reunion.

jokes.text: I guess we all have a lot of catching up to do. Let's see, where do I begin? Well, when I was 6, we all moved from the hood... And here I am today. Excuse me.
jokes.setup: Carlton offers to catch up on 14 years
jokes.punchline: Delivers entire life story in one sentence then leaves
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist comedy - Carlton's privileged perspective reduces 14 years to 'moved from the hood, here I am.' Great economy and character work.

jokes.text: When did you get so tall? / Mostly during the '80s.
jokes.setup: Father asking about son's growth during their separation
jokes.punchline: Will's deadpan chronological response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy and wit. Will deflects emotional weight with perfect deadpan timing. 'Mostly during the '80s' is absurd yet logical.

jokes.text: Wow, 14 years. You must feel like you're on a roller coaster of emotions.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to be emotionally supportive
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly clichéd pop psychology
jokes.explanation: Carlton's attempt at emotional intelligence comes out as therapy-speak cliché, showing his awkwardness with real emotion.

jokes.text: Let me hold you. [presumably awkward hug]
jokes.setup: Carlton's emotional support attempt
jokes.punchline: Forced physical affection
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward hug comedy, saved by Carlton's earnest delivery of the therapeutic gesture.

jokes.text: Still a stone cold fox.
jokes.setup: Lou reuniting with his ex-sister-in-law
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate compliment using outdated slang
jokes.explanation: Shows Lou's character - inappropriate boundaries and dated vernacular. Cringe humor from social awkwardness.

jokes.text: I asked Geoffrey to tell you. I thought it might be more fun this way. And I was right.
jokes.setup: Will explaining why he didn't warn Vivian about Lou's visit
jokes.punchline: Claiming the obvious chaos was intentional entertainment
jokes.explanation: Will's deflection through false bravado. The ironic claim that family chaos is 'fun' works as character defense mechanism.

jokes.text: I stopped on the way home. I had a few drinks. I'm kidding, I'm kidding. I'm fine, I'm fine.
jokes.setup: Vivian's concern about Will's emotional state
jokes.punchline: Will jokes about drinking then quickly clarifies
jokes.explanation: Will using humor to deflect serious emotional concern. The quick backtrack shows he knows the joke went too far.

jokes.text: If I can stop him from coming home.
jokes.setup: Vivian saying Phil will be cool with Lou's visit
jokes.punchline: Immediately revealing her plan to prevent Phil from coming home
jokes.explanation: Great subversive humor - Vivian's calm delivery of her sabotage plan. Perfect economy in revealing her true strategy.

jokes.text: Will's father is a deadbeat who left him and his mom flat.
jokes.setup: Lou introducing himself as Will's father
jokes.punchline: Hilary's brutal honesty about his abandonment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary moment - her oblivious tactlessness creates maximum awkwardness. Great character integration with her tendency to speak without filter.

jokes.text: Oh, that was fun.
jokes.setup: The awkward introduction scene
jokes.punchline: Vivian's dry assessment of the disaster
jokes.explanation: Classic deadpan reaction to social disaster. Great economy - three words capture the entire awkward situation.

jokes.text: Why is there a truck parked in my...? What are you doing here?
jokes.setup: Phil comes home to find Lou there
jokes.punchline: Phil's confusion turning to immediate hostility
jokes.explanation: Good escalation as Phil's mundane question becomes hostile recognition

jokes.text: Who knows if we'd ever see you again? Come on.
jokes.setup: Lou wanting to leave to avoid conflict
jokes.punchline: Vivian's pointed comment about his tendency to disappear
jokes.explanation: Sharp passive-aggressive dig disguised as hospitality. Vivian weaponizes politeness to call out Lou's pattern of abandonment.

jokes.text: You mean he's your... / Yeah, that's right. He's my Lou.
jokes.setup: Ashley starting to ask about the family relationship
jokes.punchline: Will's possessive twist on 'my dad' to 'my Lou'
jokes.explanation: Will's emotional deflection through wordplay. Using first name instead of 'dad' shows his protective distance while maintaining humor.

jokes.text: Then why'd you run out on him?
jokes.setup: Lou holding baby Nicky tenderly
jokes.punchline: Ashley's brutal directness about abandonment
jokes.explanation: Devastating in its simplicity. Ashley's child-like directness cuts through all adult pretense. Perfect timing and character integration.

jokes.text: Why didn't you send any back to the wife and child you abandoned?
jokes.setup: Lou bragging about making money
jokes.punchline: Phil's pointed question about financial responsibility
jokes.explanation: Phil's righteous anger delivered as cutting logic. Great character moment showing his protective nature and moral standards.

jokes.text: Shall I call 911?
jokes.setup: Phil storming out in anger
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's dry offer to call emergency services
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey moment - his dry British wit treating family drama like a medical emergency. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Please, you was up there screaming so loud... I thought I was sitting next to Patti LaBelle.
jokes.setup: Will denying fear of roller coaster
jokes.punchline: Comparison to famous singer known for powerful vocals
jokes.explanation: Great pop culture reference with perfect specificity. Patti LaBelle comparison is both accurate (loud) and absurd (roller coaster context).

jokes.text: Okay, fine. He's puffed up like a blowfish and bald as an egg.
jokes.setup: Vivian initially complimenting Lou's looks to her mother
jokes.punchline: Her honest brutal description when pressed
jokes.explanation: Vivid visual imagery with great rhythm. 'Blowfish and egg' is memorable alliteration that paints a clear picture.

jokes.text: Were your ears burning, sir?
jokes.setup: Lou arriving just as Vivian insults his appearance
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's polite inquiry about the obvious timing
jokes.explanation: Classic Geoffrey diplomatic sarcasm. Perfect timing acknowledgment delivered with formal politeness.

jokes.text: Fourteen. / Excuse me, 14 years.
jokes.setup: Philip's anger about 15 years of abandonment
jokes.punchline: Will's correction and Phil's sarcastic acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Shows Will still defending his father even during Phil's righteous anger. The precision correction reveals Will's been counting exactly.

jokes.text: The master lives a life of ease / While we live life upon our knees / Waste not your tears / And save your breath / For soon he'll die a violent death
jokes.setup: Geoffrey offering comfort with a childhood poem
jokes.punchline: The poem is about violent revenge against the wealthy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversive comedy. Geoffrey's 'comforting' childhood poem is actually revolutionary class warfare. Perfect character integration showing his hidden resentment.

jokes.text: Works for me.
jokes.setup: The violent revolutionary poem
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's casual endorsement
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan follow-up. Two words that confirm Geoffrey's secret revolutionary sympathies. Maximum comedy per word.

jokes.text: Daddy, I had a little accident at the mall. I'm okay. But I totaled your MasterCard.
jokes.setup: Hilary reporting a concerning 'accident'
jokes.punchline: The accident was overspending, not physical harm
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection and wordplay. 'Totaled' works for both car crashes and credit limits. Perfect Hilary prioritizing shopping over safety concerns.

jokes.text: He's like a genie on Valium. Make a wish, hurry.
jokes.setup: Phil being unusually accommodating
jokes.punchline: Comparison to sedated magical being
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational comedy. 'Genie on Valium' perfectly captures Phil's depressed generosity. Highly quotable and original imagery.

jokes.text: I hate you! / Oh, yeah? Well, take a number.
jokes.setup: Carlton's dramatic rejection after being denied trust fund
jokes.punchline: Phil's weary acknowledgment of widespread hatred
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of typical father-son dynamic. Phil's exhausted response suggests he's used to being the bad guy. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Here comes Mr. Sunshine. / Shut up, Geoffrey.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey's sarcastic greeting for depressed Phil
jokes.punchline: Phil's irritated response
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic butler/boss dynamic. 'Mr. Sunshine' is obvious irony but well-delivered by Geoffrey.

jokes.text: And yet, it's come back with a vengeance.
jokes.setup: Phil describing his efforts to be a good father figure
jokes.punchline: His dramatic description of teenage rebellion
jokes.explanation: Phil's melodramatic phrasing treats normal teen behavior like a horror movie sequel. Good character voice showing his tendency toward dramatic speech.

jokes.text: Somebody's being selfish all right, but it isn't Will.
jokes.setup: Phil calling Will selfish
jokes.punchline: Vivian turning the accusation back on Phil
jokes.explanation: Sharp reversal that forces Phil to examine his own motives. Vivian cuts through his self-pity with direct truth.

jokes.text: You're not gonna hit him, are you? Because I don't wanna miss that.
jokes.setup: Vivian asking Philip to take Nicky upstairs before confronting Lou
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's eagerness to witness violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey moment - polite bloodthirstiness delivered with butler formality. Great subversion of servant loyalty expectations.

jokes.text: You're not gonna hit him, are you? Because I don't wanna miss that.
jokes.setup: Vivian asks if Phil will hit Lou
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's eager anticipation of potential violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey - his bloodthirsty enthusiasm for witnessing conflict while maintaining butler propriety

jokes.text: I just wish I hadn't wasted my money buying this stupid present.
jokes.setup: Will trying to act like his father's abandonment doesn't hurt
jokes.punchline: Revealing he bought a gift, showing his true hopes
jokes.explanation: Heartbreaking character moment disguised as throwaway line. Shows Will's secret optimism and current defense mechanism through bitter humor.

tmdb_overview: Ben Vereen plays Will's long-lost father, who breezes into town and promises Will the moon, which angers an unforgiving Philip.
